title: Dry Bulk Seasonal Weakness
summary:
Main
The Baltic Dry Index retreated for the first time in over a week on Tuesday as the capesizes suffered amid weak demand across the major basins. Among the commodities, iron ore and base metals were among yesterday's losses, with concerns over the Chinese demand outlook weighing on prices. By Ulf Bergman

Macro/Geopolitics
China's consumer prices rose marginally less than expected last month.

children.title: Main
children.summary: The Baltic Dry Index retreated for the first time in over a week on Tuesday as the capesizes suffered amid weak demand across the major basins. Among the commodities, iron ore and base metals were among yesterday's losses, with concerns over the Chinese demand outlook weighing on prices.

children.title: Macro/Geopolitics
children.summary: China's consumer prices rose marginally less than expected last month. Compared to a year ago, prices rose by a meagre 0.3 per cent.

children.title: Commodity Markets
children.summary: An improving demand outlook amid the EIA's upward revision of global consumption during the year ahead contributed to continued gains for crude oil yesterday. The August Brent futures recorded a daily advance of 0.4 per cent and ended the day at 81.92 dollars per barrel, the highest closing price in nearly two weeks.

children.title: Freight and Bunker Markets
children.summary: After five consecutive sessions of gains, albeit limited in many cases, the Baltic Dry Index slipped into the red on Tuesday. The headline index recorded a daily decline of 2.8 per cent, with the capesizes providing most of the downward momentum.

children.title: The View from the Shipfix Desk
section_path: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.section_path_text: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.summary: After a few weeks of headwinds, the Baltic Exchange's panamax index has recorded five consecutive sessions of gains. The gauge declined by more than eight per cent in May but has since gained around six per cent.
